# Benchmark Dashboard {#benchmarks}

## What the Dashboard Shows You

The Benchmark Dashboard is your central window into how well the
glycoMusubi model pipeline predicts glycan structures at different levels
of specificity. It consolidates results from 33 experiments across 6
hierarchical prediction tasks into an interactive interface of
filterable tables, charts, and training-history plots. Rather than
digging through log files or spreadsheets, you can assess and compare
model performance entirely within the browser. Every element on the
page is live: tables are sortable and searchable, charts respond to
hover and click events, and experiment selections propagate instantly
across all panels.

When the module loads, four summary cards appear at the top of the
page, each delivering one key number about the best model
configuration found so far. The headline F1 (macro) score of 0.924
represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall across all
classes, giving you a balanced view of overall classification quality
that is not distorted by class imbalance. The Hits@5 value of 0.786
tells you how often the correct answer appears among the model's top
five predictions, a metric that matters greatly for retrieval-oriented
workflows where a human expert will review a short-list of candidates.
The MRR (mean reciprocal rank) of 0.066 captures the average position
of the correct answer across all test queries, penalising low ranks
more harshly than Hits@5 and thus reflecting a stricter standard.
Finally, the determination gap of 10x quantifies the performance ratio
between cluster-level tasks and exact-ID tasks, highlighting the
boundary where the model transitions from strong to weak predictions.
These four cards update automatically whenever new experiment results
are added to the data directory.

## The Hierarchical Benchmark Table

Below the summary cards sits a sortable, paginated table that breaks
performance down across six hierarchical tasks, ordered from coarsest
to finest granularity. At the coarsest level, glycan superclass
prediction distinguishes among roughly 15 candidates and achieves 0.97
accuracy with an F1 of 0.96 using only the knowledge graph. Glycan
class prediction, with roughly 80 candidates, is nearly as strong at
0.94 accuracy and 0.93 F1, still relying on the KG alone.

Moving to glycan subclass, the candidate set grows to roughly 250 and
the model begins incorporating ESM protein embeddings alongside the
KG. This hybrid approach yields an F1 of 0.92 with precision at 0.93
and recall at 0.91, showing that protein-level information adds
genuine signal at this resolution. The transition becomes sharper at
the cluster level, where roughly 1,200 candidate glycans push the
primary metric to Hits@5 at 0.79 and MRR at 0.34. Performance
reflects the fact that distinguishing among hundreds of structurally
similar clusters requires the model to capture subtle compositional
differences.

At the family level (roughly 4,500 candidates), Hits@5 drops to 0.41
and MRR to 0.09, indicating that the model can still place the correct
answer in the top five about four times out of ten, but its confidence
ranking weakens substantially. The most challenging task, exact glycan
ID prediction across roughly 58,000 candidates, manages only Hits@5 of
0.08 and MRR of 0.02. These numbers are not surprising given the
enormous output space, but they establish a clear baseline for future
improvements.

You can click any column header to sort the table or use the search
box above it to filter by task name or model type. This interactivity
makes it easy to focus on the specific task that matters most for your
research question.

## The Performance Bar Chart and the Determination Boundary

A horizontal bar chart visualises the primary metric for each of the
six tasks, offering an immediate visual summary that complements the
numerical table. The bars are colour-coded green for tasks above the
determination boundary (cluster level and coarser) and orange for
tasks below it (family and exact-ID). This colour split draws your eye
to the critical transition point in model capability.

```r
plotly::plot_ly(
  data = bench_summary,
  x = ~value, y = ~task,
  type = "bar", orientation = "h",
  color = ~above_boundary,
  colors = c("above" = "#2ca02c", "below" = "#ff7f0e")
) %>%
  layout(yaxis = list(categoryorder = "array",
                      categoryarray = rev(bench_summary$task)))
```

A dashed vertical line on the chart marks the determination
boundary --- the point where performance drops sharply. Understanding
this boundary is central to interpreting the benchmark results. The
10x determination gap means that when the number of candidate glycans
grows by roughly an order of magnitude (from roughly 1,200 clusters to
roughly 4,500 families), prediction quality degrades
disproportionately rather than declining linearly. This is a
biological reality: fine-grained glycan identity depends on subtle
enzymatic differences --- variations in branching, linkage position,
and terminal modification --- that the current feature set cannot
fully resolve. Tasks to the right of that dashed line are considered
practically solvable with the current model, while those to the left
represent open challenges that need additional data sources or
architectural innovations.

## Comparing Experiments Side by Side

The dashboard provides a multi-select dropdown listing all 33
available experiments, each named by its configuration string (for
example, `KG_TransE_dim256_lr1e-3`). Selecting two or more experiments
populates a side-by-side comparison table with one row per experiment
and columns for every recorded metric: MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, Hits@5,
Hits@10, F1 (macro), Accuracy, Precision, and Recall. This table
makes it easy to evaluate trade-offs between model architectures,
embedding dimensions, and learning rates at a glance.

```r
selected_ids <- input$experiment_selector
comp_df <- all_results %>%
  filter(experiment_id %in% selected_ids) %>%
  select(experiment_id, MRR, `Hits@1`, `Hits@5`,
         F1, Accuracy, Precision, Recall) %>%
  arrange(desc(MRR))

DT::datatable(comp_df, options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE))
```

Sorting by MRR lets you quickly identify the best-performing
configuration overall. You should then check whether Hits@5 and F1
agree with that ranking, since divergence between ranking-based and
classification-based metrics can reveal interesting model behaviour. A
model that scores well on MRR but poorly on F1 may be good at placing
the correct answer near the top of the list but unreliable when forced
to make a hard classification decision. Conversely, high F1 with low
MRR suggests the model makes correct binary predictions but struggles
with the ranking task.

## Inspecting Training History

Choosing an experiment from the training-history dropdown reveals two
panels that together tell the story of how that model learned. The
left panel plots training loss (blue) and validation loss (orange)
against epoch number. The point where validation loss levels off or
begins to increase indicates the optimal stopping epoch. If the gap
between training and validation loss grows steadily over time, that is
a clear sign of overfitting --- the model is memorising the training
set rather than learning generalisable patterns.

The right panel shows validation MRR and Hits@5 at each checkpoint
epoch, with a vertical grey line marking the epoch that was ultimately
selected for final evaluation. Together, these curves let you verify
that training converged cleanly. A well-behaved run shows smooth,
monotonically improving metric curves that plateau near the checkpoint
marker, with no oscillation or sudden drops.

```r
plot_ly(history, x = ~epoch) %>%
  add_trace(y = ~train_loss, name = "Train loss",
            line = list(color = "#1f77b4")) %>%
  add_trace(y = ~val_loss, name = "Val loss",
            line = list(color = "#ff7f0e")) %>%
  layout(xaxis = list(title = "Epoch"),
         yaxis = list(title = "Loss"))
```

## A Practical Workflow for Finding the Best Model

Start by opening the Benchmark Dashboard tab and reviewing the
top-level metric cards for a quick summary of the current best
results. Then scan the hierarchical benchmark table, focusing on the
task most relevant to your research. If you are interested in glycan
cluster prediction, for instance, look at the Hits@5 and MRR values
for that row and note which model type (KG-only or KG+ESM) was used.

Next, select three to five experiments that target your task of
interest in the comparison dropdown, and sort the resulting table by
your preferred metric. Once you have identified the top candidate,
click through to its training history and verify that training
converged cleanly --- no oscillation, no early divergence, no signs of
overfitting. Record the winning experiment ID, because you will need
it when working in the Embedding Explorer or Hierarchy Browser to
understand what the model actually learned.

## Practical Tips

Metric choice matters more than it might seem at first glance. MRR
penalises low ranks harshly, making it the right choice when the
position of the correct answer in a ranked list is critical, whereas
Hits@5 is more lenient and better suited for classification-oriented
use cases where you simply need the correct answer to appear somewhere
in a short-list. Choosing the wrong metric for your application can
lead you to select a model that performs well by one standard but
poorly by the one that actually matters.

Every bar and line in the dashboard plots shows exact values on hover,
so you never need to estimate from axis positions. The DT table
includes a CSV export button that lets you download comparison results
for external analysis in Excel, Python, or any other tool. For full
reproducibility, each experiment ID maps to a configuration YAML in
`experiments/configs/` that you can load with
`yaml::read_yaml("experiments/configs/<id>.yaml")` to inspect every
hyperparameter --- learning rate, embedding dimension, negative
sampling ratio, and all other settings that produced those results.
